SAP IAG administrators can download the standard notification template package directly from the application. This export contains all notification workflow templates—access request creation, approval, rejection, provisioning success/failure, privileged access events, and more.

Navigation Path

You can download the standard template by following the path:

Administration → Template Upload → Notification → Standard Template: Download

2. Downloaded File

The downloaded ZIP file contains:

  • A comprehensive JSON file with all SAP‑delivered notification workflow templates.
  • Each template includes:
    • Subject line
    • Recipient structure (to/cc/bcc)
    • HTML body
    • Context‑based variables (e.g., "${context.requestId}, ${context.userName}, ${context.link}")

Editing this JSON directly is technically possible, but time‑consuming and error‑prone due to:

  • Escaped characters,
  • Embedded HTML,
  • Nested workflow metadata,
  • Potential corruption of system variables.

To solve this, here is a guide for IAG Notification Customization, which extracts editable areas from the JSON and highlights them clearly.

3. SAP IAG Notification Template - Customization Guide

This document identifies the sections in your SAP Identity Access Governance (IAG) notification templates where you can safely add custom content. For each template, you will typically customize: Subject, Header/Intro line, Body text (within the HTML <body>), Footer/sign-off, and optional branding (logo). Variables like ${context.*} must be preserved.

Legend: Text in Green Font = recommended area to edit; variables must remain intact.

a. Template: notifyapprover_en

Subject: Access request ${context.requestId} requires your decision

To: ${context.to}

CC: ${context.cc}

BCC: ${context.bcc}

Editable Body (inside <body>):

<!--<h3><img src="logo.png" alt="Logo"></h3>-->
<p>Dear approver,</p>
<p></p>
<p>A new access request ${context.requestId} requires your approval. Please check your <a href="${context.link}">request inbox app</a> (SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance) for the access request ${context.requestId}.</p>
<p>This message is automatically generated and for your information only.  Please do not reply.</p>
<p>Best regards</p>

b. Template: notifyrequestapproved_en

Subject: Privileged access request ${context.requestId} has been successfully approved

To: ${context.to}

CC: ${context.cc}

BCC: ${context.bcc}

Editable Body (inside <body>):

<!--<h3><img src="logo.png" alt="Logo"></h3>-->
<p>Dear ${context.userName},</p>
<p></p>
<p>Privileged access request ${context.requestId} has been successfully approved.
You can check its status in the <a href="${context.link}">Access Request Status</a> app (SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance).</p>
<p>This message is automatically generated and for your information only.</p>
<p>Best regards</p>

c. Template: notifyuserpartialprovisioned_en

Subject: Access Request ${context.requestId} was completed with errors

To: ${context.to}

CC: ${context.cc}

BCC: ${context.bcc}

Editable Body (inside <body>):

<!--<h3><img src="logo.png" alt="Logo"></h3>-->
<p>Dear ${context.userName},</p>
<p></p>
<p>Your access request ${context.requestId} has been completed with errors. For more information, please check its status in the Access Request Status app (SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance) or contact your system administrator.</p>
<p>This message is automatically generated and for your information only.</p>
<p>Best regards</p>

d. Template: notifyrequestcreated_en

Subject: Your access request ${context.requestId} has been successfully submitted

To: ${context.to}

CC: ${context.cc}

BCC: ${context.bcc}

Editable Body (inside <body>):

<!--<h3><img src="logo.png" alt="Logo"></h3>-->
<p>Dear ${context.userName},</p>
<p></p>
<p>Access request ${context.requestId} has been successfully submitted for approval.
You can check its status in the <a href="${context.link}">Access Request Status</a> app (SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance).</p>
<p>This message is automatically generated and for your information only.</p>
<p>Best regards</p>

e. Template: notifyrequestrejected_en

Subject: Your access request ${context.requestId} has been rejected

To: ${context.to}

CC: ${context.cc}

BCC: ${context.bcc}

Editable Body (inside <body>):

<!--<h3><img src="logo.png" alt="Logo"></h3>-->
 <p>Dear ${context.userName},</p>
<p></p>
 <p>The access request ${context.requestId} that you submitted has been rejected. You can check its details in the Access Request Status app (SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance).</p>
<p>This message is automatically generated and for your information only. </p>
<p>Best regards</p>

f. Template: notifyuserprovisioned_en

Subject: Your requested access in request ${context.requestId} has been successfully completed

To: ${context.to}

CC: ${context.cc}

BCC: ${context.bcc}

Editable Body (inside <body>):

<p>Dear ${context.userName},</p>
<p></p>
<p>You have been granted access to the requested application. You can check its details in the Access Request Status app (SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance).</p>
<p>This message is automatically generated and for your information only.</p>
<p>Best regards</p>

g. Template: notifyuserprovisionfailed_en

Subject: Access Request ${context.requestId} failed provisioning

To: ${context.to}

CC: ${context.cc}

BCC: ${context.bcc}

Editable Body (inside <body>):

<!--<h3><img src="logo.png" alt="Logo"></h3>-->
<p>Dear ${context.userName},</p>
<p></p>
<p>Your access request in request ${context.requestId} could not be completed at this time. For more information, please check its status in the Access Request Status app (SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance).</p>
<p>This message is automatically generated and for your information only.</p>
<p>Best regards</p> 

h. Template: notifyrequestcancelled_en

Subject: Your access request ${context.requestId} has been cancelled

To: ${context.to}

CC: ${context.cc}

BCC: ${context.bcc}

Editable Body (inside <body>):

<!--<h3><img src="logo.png" alt="Logo"></h3>-->
 <p>Dear ${context.userName},</p>
<p></p>
 <p>Access request ${context.requestId} has been cancelled by administrator.
You can check its status in the <a href="${context.link}">Access Request Status</a> app (SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance).</p>
<p>This message is automatically generated and for your information only.</p>
<p>Best regards</p>

i. Template: notifypamreview_en

Subject: Review Request ${context.requestId} requires your review

To: ${context.to}

CC: ${context.cc}

BCC: ${context.bcc}

Editable Body (inside <body>):

<!--<h3><img src="logo.png" alt="Logo"></h3>-->
<p>Dear ${context.reviewerName},</p>
<p></p>
 <p>Privileged Access Management session ${context.pamObjectId} started by ${context.executorName} in application ${context.application} was terminated. Please review the logs at your earliest convenience in <a href="${context.link}">Privileged Access Review Inbox</a> application.</p>
<p>This message is automatically generated and for your information only.  Please do not reply.</p>
<p>Best regards</p>

j. Template: notifypamapprove_en

Subject: Privileged Access Request ${context.requestId} requires your approval

To: ${context.to}

CC: ${context.cc}

BCC: ${context.bcc}

Editable Body (inside <body>):

<!--<h3><img src="logo.png" alt="Logo"></h3>-->
<p>Dear ${context.approverName},</p>
<p></p>
 <p>Privileged Access request ${context.requestId} for ${context.pamObjectId} requires your approval. Please make your decision at your earliest convenience in <a href="${context.link}">Privileged Access Inbox</a> application.</p>
<p>This message is automatically generated and for your information only.  Please do not reply.</p>
<p>Best regards</p>

k. Template: notifypamrequestapproved_en

Subject: Privileged access request ${context.requestId} has been successfully approved

To: ${context.to}

CC: ${context.cc}

BCC: ${context.bcc}

Editable Body (inside <body>):

<!--<h3><img src="logo.png" alt="Logo"></h3>-->
<p>Dear ${context.fullName},</p>
<p></p>
<p>Privileged access request ${context.requestId} has been successfully approved.
You can check its status in the <a href="${context.link}">Access Request Status</a> app (SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance).</p>
<p>This message is automatically generated and for your information only.</p>
<p>Best regards</p>

l. Template: notifypamrequestsubmitted_en

Subject: Privileged access request ${context.requestId} has been successfully submitted for approval

To: ${context.to}

CC: ${context.cc}

BCC: ${context.bcc}

Editable Body (inside <body>):

<!--<h3><img src="logo.png" alt="Logo"></h3>-->
<p>Dear ${context.fullName},</p>
<p></p>
<p>Privileged access request ${context.requestId} for ${context.pamObjectId} has been successfully submitted for approval.
You can check its status in the <a href="${context.link}">Access Request Status</a> app (SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance).</p>
<p>This message is automatically generated and for your information only.</p>
<p>Best regards</p>

m. Template: notifypamrequestrejected_en

Subject: Privileged access request ${context.requestId} has been rejected

To: ${context.to}

CC: ${context.cc}

BCC: ${context.bcc}

Editable Body (inside <body>):

<!--<h3><img src="logo.png" alt="Logo"></h3>-->
<p>Dear ${context.fullName},</p>
<p></p>
<p>Privileged access request ${context.requestId} for ${context.pamObjectId} has been rejected.
You can check its status in the <a href="${context.link}">Access Request Status</a> app (SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance).</p>
<p>This message is automatically generated and for your information only.</p>
<p>Best regards</p>

n. Template: notifypamrequestforward_en

Subject: Privileged Access Requests forwarded to you for approving

To: ${context.to}

CC: ${context.cc}

BCC: ${context.bcc}

Editable Body (inside <body>):

<p>Dear ${context.userName},</p>

<p></p>

<p>Privileged access management log requests have been forwarded to you for review: ${context.requestIds}</p>

<p>Please review the logs at your earliest convenience in <a href="${context.link}">Privileged Access Review Inbox</a> application.</p>

<p>This message is automatically generated and for your information only. Please do not reply. </p>
<p>Best regards</p>

o. Template: notifypamrequestcancelled_en

Subject: Privileged access request ${context.requestId} has been cancelled

To: ${context.to}

CC: ${context.cc}

BCC: ${context.bcc}

Editable Body (inside <body>):

<!--<h3><img src="logo.png" alt="Logo"></h3>-->
 <p>Dear ${context.fullName},</p>
<p></p>
<p>Privileged access request ${context.requestId} for ${context.pamObjectId} has been cancelled.
You can check its status in the <a href="${context.link}">Access Request Status</a> app (SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance).</p>
<p>This message is automatically generated and for your information only.</p>
<p>Best regards</p>

Important Tips:

- Keep placeholders like ${context.requestId}, ${context.link}, ${context.userName} unchanged.

- You can add branding (e.g., <img src="logo.png">) by un-commenting the logo line or replacing the image link.

- Maintain HTML structure; avoid removing closing tags.

 

4. Customize and Upload Back to SAP IAG

  • Edit the JSON Using the Guide
    • Open the ZIP → extract the JSON.
    • Refer to highlighted areas in the guide.
    • Update only highlight text in the JSON.
  • Save and Upload
    • Save the updated JSON.
    • Upload the JSON file only. Do not zip it.
    • Navigate to: Administration → Template Upload → Notification → Template Archive → Upload

 

  • Check the Processing Status and Process Log to ensure successful import.

Important Note:

The above guide just showing the areas which can be customized and not the complete JSON script from the standard template. You can identity and focus only on the highlighted text in standard JSON script while updating. Ensure not to change any other content from standard template.

entry.title: User Filtering for Repository Synchronization in SAP Identity Access Governance
entry.content:
Introduction:

In the Standard Edition of SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance (IAG), subscription consumption is calculated using the Resources metric. This metric represents the number of unique users, including technical users, synchronized from on‑premise and/or cloud systems into SAP IAG.

More information available on: SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance - License Metric 

3262845 - How to monitor IAG usage? 

In many customers’ landscapes, not all users maintained in target systems require identity governance. Test users, technical users, and generic accounts typically do not participate in provisioning, access risk analysis, or access certification. Synchronizing such users into SAP IAG may unnecessarily increase subscription consumption without delivering business value.

To address this, SAP IAG provides user filtering capabilities during repository synchronization, enabling customers to limit synchronization to governance‑relevant users only. This blog outlines the available filtering options across common SAP IAG integration scenarios.

Integration Scenarios in SAP IAG Standard Edition

Depending on the target application, SAP IAG supports the following integration models:

  1. On‑premise SAP ABAP systems
    Connected via SAP Cloud Connector
    Examples: SAP ECC, SAP S/4HANA (On‑Premise)
  2. SAP cloud applications integrated using SAP Identity Provisioning Service (IPS)
    Connected through a proxy system
    Examples: SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS), SAP Ariba, SAP Analytics Cloud
  3. SAP cloud applications integrated directly using SAP BTP destinations
    Examples: SAP Concur, SAP IBP, SAP Marketing Cloud, SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud

Each integration model offers distinct options for filtering users during repository synchronization.

1. User Filtering for Applications Integrated via SAP IPS Proxy

For applications integrated through SAP Identity Provisioning Service (IPS), user filtering is implemented using transformation rules in the proxy system. These rules determine which users are synchronized into SAP IAG during repository sync.

Example: Filtering Users from SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS)

In this scenario, users are filtered based on IAS user group membership:

  • A group named Z_SAP_IAG_TEST is maintained in SAP IAS.
  • An IPS transformation rule is configured to synchronize only users assigned to this group.
  • During repository synchronization, only users belonging to this group are synced into SAP IAG, while all other IAS users are excluded.

This approach ensures that only relevant users are onboarded into SAP IAG.

Currently there are 16 users assigned to this group in SAP IAS.

On execution of repository object sync in SAP IAG for SAP IAS, only 16 users got synchronized.

Sample Transformation Filter Expressions

Filters can be defined using various user attributes, depending on the application. Below are few examples:

SAP IAS

ias.user.filter = userName eq "john.doe"
ias.user.filter = userName co "admin"
ias.user.filter = groups.display eq "Finance_Team" or groups.display eq "HR_Team"

SAP Analytics Cloud

sac.user.filter = name.familyName eq "Smith" and addresses.country eq "Germany"
sac.user.filter = userName sw "SAP_"

SAP Ariba

ariba.applications.user.filter = userName eq "SmithJ"
ariba.applications.user.filter = name.familyName eq "Johnson" and addresses.country eq "USA"
ariba.applications.user.filter = userName sw "PROC_"

These filters allow precise control over which users are synchronized based on naming patterns, attributes, or group assignments.

2. User Filtering for Applications Integrated via BTP Destination or Cloud Connector

Some SAP cloud applications do not require an IPS proxy and can be integrated directly using SAP BTP destinations.

Similarly, on‑premise ABAP systems first establish connectivity through SAP Cloud Connector, followed by destination configuration in the BTP subaccount.

After integration, each target system is represented as an Application in SAP IAG under Administration.

Using the User Filter Option in SAP IAG

SAP IAG provides a User Filter option at the application level:

  • Filters are defined using attributes such as User ID, User Group, or User Type (availability depends on the application).
  • Currently, SAP IAG supports exclude‑only filtering.
  • Users matching the exclusion criteria are not synchronized into SAP IAG during repository sync.

 

 Example: Excluding Users from SAP Concur

In this example:

  • Specific Concur user IDs are configured in the exclusion filter.
  • Multiple exclusion conditions can be added.
  • During repository synchronization, excluded users are no longer synchronized into SAP IAG.

This approach is effective for excluding technical or generic users across recurring synchronizations.

On‑Premise ABAP Systems (ECC / S/4HANA)

For SAP ECC and SAP S/4HANA (On‑Premise):

  • Exclusion filters can be applied using User ID, User Group and User Type.
  • For advanced requirements, SAP provides an enhancement option via enhancement spot in SIAG_USER_GET_LIST function module and BADI implementation.

Relevant SAP Note:2802767 - Enhancement for filtering users in SAP ECC and S/4 HANA OP sync to IAG 

3. User Filtering for SAP SuccessFactors

SAP SuccessFactors offers a dedicated filtering mechanism using application parameters in SAP IAG.

Configuration Details

  • Navigate to Administration -> Configuration application in SAP IAG
    • Configuration Group: SUCCESSFACTORS_SYNC
    • Parameter Name: USER_FILTER
    • Parameter Value: condition for different attributes and values.

Filtering is based on attributes exposed by the SuccessFactors OData API.

Examples of Filter Conditions

1. Synchronize only active employees:

Parameter value: status eq 't'

2. Synchronize only permanent employees:

empInfo/JobInfoNav/employmentType eq '3637'

If the attribute is not the top-level service, OData navigation paths must be used. Here the navigation is empInfo-> JobInfoNav- > employmentType

 Multiple conditions can be combined using the ‘and’ operator.

Refer SAP document for more information: Application Parameters | SAP help portal

 

Conclusion

User filtering during repository synchronization is a key capability in SAP Identity Access Governance that helps customers:

  • Optimize subscription consumption
  • Avoid onboarding non-governance‑relevant users
  • Maintain a lean and efficient identity landscape

By using IPS transformation rules, application‑level exclusion filters, enhancement options for on‑premise systems, and SuccessFactors application parameters, customers can align SAP IAG synchronization precisely with their governance requirements.

entry.title: Migrate Data from SAP IDM to Anywhere
entry.content:
Every SAP IDM migration project eventually reaches a point where all workflows in the new IAM have been reviewed, all interfaces have been tested, and all the useful side jobs which have never been core to IAM, but which SAP IDM used to handle so effortlessly, have been moved to someone else's scope. At that point, you might ask yourself: “How do we get our actual data from SAP IDM into the new system?”

In this article, I will discuss IDM CSV Server, a data migration tool for use with SAP Identity Management which I have worked on for the last couple of months. IDM CSV Server is fully agnostic of which specific target IAM vendor or product you have opted to migrate to. To emphasize that this discussion is applicable to any migration target, I’ll use „NextGen“ from here on as a generic alias for referring to your next generation IAM solution.

DISCLAIMER: IDM CSV Server is free and open source available under an Apache License 2.0. It is not endorsed or supported by SAP. I am not an employee of SAP or affiliated with SAP in any other way.

IDM CSV Server's releases, documentation, source code and bug tracker can be found on the project's landing page at:

https://codeberg.org/freicoder/idm-server-csv

IDM CSV Server's latest release is always available for download from the the following stable link:

https://codeberg.org/freicoder/idm-server-csv/releases/latest

IDM CSV Server Architecture

The following block diagram illustrates IDM CSV Server’s architecture in the context of SAP IDM and NextGen:

 

The key point in IDM CSV Server’s architecture is that it provides a direct and secure communication path between SAP IDM and NextGen. Using any RESTful client such as the one included in IDM CSV Server’s distribution, NextGen pulls new data from SAP IDM via HTTPS whenever it needs to. Requests are initiated by the client, running on or near NextGen, and served by SAP IDM’s REST API, running on SAP NetWeaver.

In contrast to IDM CSV Server, traditional data migration schemes often require:

  • direct “database sync” connections initiated from NextGen to read from the SAP IDM Database
  • export CSV to SAP IDM runtime file system; forward to NextGen via file server or SFTP
  • ad-hoc queries from developer workstation; forward to NextGen out-of-band/manually

Unlike many of these schemes, IDM CSV Server neither requires that your SAP IDM Runtime take on a file server role, nor does it require opening firewalls for direct access from NextGen to SAP IDM Database and/or SAP IDM Runtime. All communication takes place from NextGen to SAP NetWeaver via HTTPS exclusively, which helps to maintain both SAP IDM’s and NextGen's strong security posture.

Unlike ad-hoc queries executed interactively, IDM CSV Server operations can be fully automated and require no human in the loop. This is particularly important if you need to migrate incrementally, e.g. company by company or application by application. Such scenarios may require operating SAP IDM and NextGen in parallel for a while, increasing the relevance of IDM CSV Server’s capability to automate and repeat the full data migration cycle as often as you need.

Migrate Wherever You Want

IDM CSV Server is distributed as a ZIP archive containing both server-side components to be deployed on SAP IDM and client-side components to be deployed on or near NextGen. As you may expect, running integrated into SAP IDM implies that IDM CSV Server’s server-side aims to be compatible with any database and operating system supported by SAP IDM. IDM CSV Server’s client-side, on the other hand, is a Linux shell script designed to be compatible with many Linux distributions.

“Wait, does that mean you only support NextGen running on Linux?”

Corporate IT departments in general and SAP customers in particular have an undeniable preference for Microsoft Windows. IAM vendors naturally tailor their products to this reality. So if your specific choice of NextGen makes it difficult or impractical to run Linux shell scripts, don’t worry - developing your own custom client for IDM CSV Server is pretty simple.

To make the task as easy as possible, IDM CSV Server comes with an HTTP request collection for Bruno, an open source REST API client. Bruno is a developer tool specifically designed for prototyping and testing REST APIs - very much like Postman. Using IDM CSV Server’s Bruno collection and the official Linux client as a blueprint, any IT professional - possibly equipped with some GenAI coding assistance - can build their own client in whatever language they prefer, be it PowerShell, Python, Node.js or others.

Export What You Need

“How do we make sure the CSV contains everything we need?”

Knowing which parts exactly of SAP IDM’s data must be migrated to NextGen requires in-depth understanding of your IAM scenario; there’s no single answer applicable to all. The good news is that IDM CSV Server includes plenty of content designed to make your conceptual task easier. More importantly, it does not require you to cram everything into one single CSV.

CSV Export Types

IDM CSV Server has a concept of so-called CSV Export Types, or export types for short. Each export type comes as an SAP IDM package, which can be deployed separately as needed. Export types help you organize your SAP IDM data into multiple CSV files properly, each with its own structure and purpose. The key elements of each export type are:

  1. unique name of export type
  2. database query for the input
  3. attribute mapping for the CSV output

IDM CSV Server includes a number of ready-made export types corresponding one-to-one to SAP IDM entry types, plus the special ASSIGNMENT export type:

 

CSV Export TypeSAP IDM Entry Type
PERSONMX_PERSON
ROLEMX_ROLE
PRIVILEGEMX_PRIVILEGE
ASSIGNMENTn/a

Hence, you just need to pick and choose to have all standard attributes of several standard SAP IDM entry types at your disposal with zero effort. However, it’s important to understand that export types are not limited to SAP IDM entry types. Any result set from any SQL query can be an export type, making IDM CSV Server as flexible as traditional “database sync” options.

Customization

If your SAP IDM schema contains custom attributes that you wish to migrate, all you need to do is add these custom attributes to IDM CSV Server’s existing export types. If you have custom entry types as well, creating dedicated new export types for them is equally straight-forward. You’ll need SAP IDM Developer Studio to copy packages and modify job passes, but nothing more - no custom coding in JavaScript or Java is required.

The following diagram illustrates the process of creating a custom export type -a one-time development activity- and then generating and downloading the resulting CSV data at runtime. The latter can be executed once or scheduled to run periodically in case you need to transfer updates from SAP IDM to NextGen repeatedly.

Reference Attributes Quirks

When evaluating IDM CSV Server, you’ll notice that out-of-the-box export types, such as PERSON, only contain regular attributes such as MX_FIRSTNAME and MX_LASTNAME, but no reference attributes. MXREF_MX_ROLE, MXREF_MX_PRIVILEGE and the like are all excluded from CSV, which might seem counter-intuitive at first. Knowing which identity is assigned to which roles and/or privileges is, after all, key to IAM’s core business. Everyone will need that information in NextGen, with no exceptions.

This is where the built-in export type ASSIGNMENT comes in. ASSIGNMENT is basically a one-to-one copy of SAP IDM’s database view IDMV_LINK_EXT, with all details like direct/indirect, assignment status and more included. Hence, IDM CSV Server by default suggests to migrate assignments in a format where each assignment is a single CSV row.

This is in sharp contrast to the alternative of including assignments directly in their respective entry type’s CSV, such as that of PERSON, and representing MXREF attributes as multi-value columns with single values separated by a separator character.

Having a dedicated export type ASSIGNMENT has two main benefits:

  1. Zero information loss: all assignment properties from SAP IDM remain at your disposal in NextGen
  2. Superior performance: requires constant memory and linear runtime for any data volume

As always, flexibility comes at a cost. Evaluating your migrated identities’ role assignments, for instance, requires that you import three CSV files (PERSON, ASSIGNMENT and ROLE) into NextGen and then perform a three way join

PERSON > ASSIGNMENT > ROLE

much in the same way as you would query the SAP IDM Database using an SQL join:

IDMV_ENTRY_SIMPLE > IDMV_LINK_EXT > IDMV_ENTRY_SIMPLE

With that said, there’s nothing that stops you from including reference attributes directly in your own export types or from adding them to IDM CSV Server’s out-of-the-box export types if you need to.

Each export type package comes with a switch FX_USE_FASTTRACK that you can toggle to switch from the default, SQL-based data retrieval to a more generic, identity store-based data retrieval. With the latter option, adding reference attributes is as simple as adding a new line to the load job’s attribute mapping. No changes to the database query are required.

Where to Go from Here?

If you’re an SAP IDM user and wondering whether IDM CSV Server could help you migrate to NextGen more easily, feel free to install and evaluate IDM CSV Server in your development system. Installing all parts of IDM CSV Server is extensively documented in the user manual, a must read for anyone considering to try out IDM CSV Server.

As with all software, IDM CSV Server contains bugs and I cannot rule out that you'll run into one of them when using IDM CSV Server. Should this happen, please use the project’s bug tracker on Codeberg to report your issue.

Please do not use the comment section of this post for reporting bugs. SAP Community is a great place to discuss, but it is definitely inadequate for tracking software issues and their resolutions.

What’s Next(Gen)?

This article has focused on the old world: “How do I get data out of SAP IDM?” Much more interesting, however, is the follow-up question: “How do I get it into NextGen?”

Answering the latter question will require a separate, product-specific discussion for each relevant migration target. Generic aliases like "NextGen" be gone, time to talk about specific IAM products available on the market today. Of course, no one knows every IAM vendor inside out. As for myself, I'll hence limit myself to writing about one or two of them in the foreseeable future and cover those in depth, rather than merely skim the surface of many.

I suppose that you, my dear reader, might be an expert yourself in one of those IAM solutions that aim to succeed SAP IDM. It doesn't matter whether your candidate is established or niche, on-prem or cloud, open source or proprietary. If you're interested in publishing about data migration from SAP IDM to yours with the help of IDM CSV Server - definitely get in touch! I'd be delighted to contribute and collaborate.

Let me conclude this post on a personal and thankful note: Farewell, Data Synchronisation Engine. You've been fun to work with.

entry.title: Automate User Lifecycle Management [SAP IAS]: Block or Delete with Confidence
entry.content:
Common Use case across IT Landscape :
Users who have not logged into SAP applications for the last 90 days to be automatically set to Block status.  IT administrators and application owners to easily identify inactive or unused accounts, improving security visibility and control.
 
Introducing this feature for SAP IAS - Tenant administrator can now configure specific user types to be blocked and deleted, or only blocked, or only deleted after a certain period of inactivity.
 
The users for which blocking can be enabled are of the type:
  • Public
  • Customers
  • Partners
  • Employees
You can enable the chosen user type or types to be only blocked, or only deleted, or both blocked and deleted after a certain period of inactivity.
The period spans from 14 up to 2000 days.

To enable blocking and or deletion, you must choose at least one user type and set at least one period.

After a user has exceeded the set period of inactivity, they will be blocked and their status will be updated on the next sign-in attempt.

You can also configure when the deletion script starts.

Steps to enable 

  1. Sign in to the administration console for SAP Cloud Identity Services.
  2. Under Applications and Resources, choose the Tenant Settings tile.

    At the top of the page, you can view the administrative and license relevant information of the tenant.

  3. Choose the Blocking and Deletion list item.
  4. Choose Edit.
  5. Select a user type.
  6. Enable the options under Inactivity Periods. By default the options are disabled. At least one of the status fields Block after or Delete after must be enabled.
How to find the Last Logon Date of the User to start the Inactivity Blocking User.

User Persona (Examples)

By configuring a 90-day inactivity rule, organizations can automatically block users who have not accessed SAP applications, allowing IT administrators and application owners to quickly identify dormant accounts and strengthen overall security governance.
Mixed User Persona for Block & Deletion
 

By tailoring inactivity rules for different user types, organizations can strike the perfect balance between security, compliance, and user experience—turning identity lifecycle management into a strategic advantage

 

Reference : https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-identity-services/cloud-identity-services/block-or-delete-users-due-to-inactivity?version=Cloud

entry.title: Application Deletion in SAP Identity Access Governance (IAG) – Practical Approach
entry.content:
Introduction

During one of the SAP Identity Access Governance (IAG) implementation projects, I encountered a common challenge related to application cleanup in test systems before UAT.

Typically, we integrate multiple SAP cloud and on-premises applications into the IAG landscape during the initial phases in the test environment, followed by reconfiguration in production after successful completion of UT, SIT, and UAT cycles.

In real project scenarios, however, the test system often ends up with additional applications created for temporary purposes such as proof of concept (POC), testing, or initial integration validation.

 

Business Challenge

Before starting UAT, clients expect a clean IAG environment without unnecessary:

  • Applications
  • Access/request data
  • Business roles
  • Users and role assignments

However, earlier SAP guidance (for example, SAP Note 2817560) indicated that applications could not be deleted once used, primarily due to audit considerations.

This created a challenge in maintaining a clean and controlled UAT environment.

 

Solution Overview

SAP has now addressed this limitation by introducing a role collection:

CIAG_Connector_Deletion_Administration

This role collection enables administrators to delete applications along with all associated data in SAP IAG.

 

Step-by-Step Approach

Step 1: Clean Up Risk Analysis Data

Before deleting an application, it is important to remove risk analysis dependencies.

  • Navigate to Administration → Applications tile
  • Select the relevant application
  • Remove the Business Function Group association

  • Run the "Access Analysis" job via Job Scheduler

This ensures removal of

  • Role-level risk analysis results
  • User-level risk analysis results

Note: This step does not delete the ruleset or business function group itself. If required, those can be deleted separately from the Business Function Groups tile.

 

Step 2: Delete the Application and Associated Data

  • Navigate to Administration → Applications tile
  • Select the application to be deleted
  • Click on Delete button at bottom right corner

  • Enter the application name for confirmation
  • Click Delete button

System Behavior

  • A message appears indicating deletion is in progress
  • After completion, the application is removed from the list
  • On refresh, the application is no longer visible

You can validate the cleanup through

  • Maintain User Data → to check user-related data
  • Access Maintenance → to verify role/access cleanup

 

Important Considerations

  • Avoid mapping this role collection to SAP IAS groups that are requestable via IAG, in case SAP IAS is also one of the target applications
  • Do not assign this role in production environments, as production data should not be deleted to comply with audit requirements.
  • In test systems:
    • Assign only temporarily
    • Remove immediately after use to prevent accidental deletion

 

Key learnings

  • Application deletion in SAP IAG is now possible with controlled authorization
  • Proper cleanup (risk data, associations) is critical before deletion
  • This feature is especially useful for:
    • UAT preparation
    • Cleaning up POC integrations
    • Maintaining governance in test systems

entry.title: Integrating SAP Concur with SAP IAG – Implementation Guide and Key Learnings
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP IAG Enterprise (Standard) edition provides out-of-the-box integration with SAP Concur for user provisioning and access governance. However, during implementation, it has been observed that certain areas require deeper understanding beyond standard documentation.

This blog covers:

  • Detailed configuration steps
  • Important technical considerations
  • Practical challenges faced during implementation

The intent is to provide a simple and practical guide for SAP IAG consultants working on SAP Concur integration.

 

Initial SAP IAG System Setup

Before starting the integration, ensure the following setup is completed:

  1. SAP BTP setup: Global Account → Subaccount → SAP IAG Enterprise Edition subscription (Test/Prod)
  2. Trust configuration is completed with SAP Cloud Identity Services (CIS), including attribute mapping
  3. Standard SAP IAG groups are created in SAP CIS, such as: IAG_USER, IAG_WF_MANAGER, IAG_WF_ADMIN, IAG_RAA_DEFAULT, etc.
  4. If the manager is used as an approval stage in access requests, the user’s manager must be assigned with the IAG_WF_MANAGER group so that it is automatically derived in the access request form. Otherwise, the requester needs to manually select the manager from users assigned to this group
  5. Role collection to CIS group mapping is configured (for example): CIAG_Access_Request, CIAG_Access_Analysis mapped to a custom CIS group like ZIAG_Access_Requestor.
  6. Access to SAP IAG with administrator privileges
  7. User data source is configured using SAP Identity Services Identity Directory or Identity Authentication Service via IPS_PROXY V2.
  8. Application parameters and master data are properly maintained
  9. User and group synchronization is configured using SCIM
  10. Mail server is configured for outbound emails (Destinations: parameters_destination and bpmworkflowruntime_mail)

 

SAP Concur to SAP IAG Integration and Configuration Steps

The following steps outline the end-to-end configuration required to integrate SAP Concur with SAP IAG after completing the initial system setup.

 

Step 1: User Provisioning Service V4 (UPSv4)

User Provisioning Service V4 (UPSv4) is an SAP Concur API framework that enables SAP IAG to automate user provisioning and lifecycle management in SAP Concur.

To use the User Provisioning Service (UPSv4) API, the client must have Concur Expense, Concur Travel, or both.

Clients using Concur Expense Professional/Premium Edition and/or Concur Request Professional/Premium Edition need to get UPS enabled through their Concur Client Executive (Sales Representative).

In addition, the client must subscribe to Client Web Services after signing the web services agreement. This will then be included as part of the SAP Concur contract.

For API details, refer to:

  • SAP API Hub – Concur User Provisioning
  • Concur Developer Documentation – User Provisioning v4

There are also certain limitations with UPS, which can be referred to in SAP Note:

  • SAP Note 3363157

 

Step 2: Configure OAuth2 authentication application and Generate company refresh token and UUID

Once the User Provisioning Service (UPSv4) is enabled, the next step is to configure OAuth2 authentication for SAP Concur integration.

This step establishes secure API-based authentication between SAP IAG and SAP Concur using OAuth2 credentials and tokens.

This involves two key activities:

Step 2.1: Configure OAuth2 Application

This helps to create authentication credentials (Client ID/Secret) and defines required API access scopes.

  • Create an OAuth2 authentication application in SAP Concur
  • Generate Client ID and Client Secret
  • Maintain the required grants and scopes

Step 2.2: Generate Company Token and UUID

This provides tenant-specific identifiers and tokens required for API communication with SAP Concur.

  • Generate the Company Request Token
  • Obtain: Token Service URL, Refresh Token, Company UUID

A detailed reference for this setup is available in the SAP Community blog by @tim_chapman24 

  • Introduction to Web Services for Concur Expense Authentication

Additional reference:

  • Concur Authentication API Documentation

Important Configuration Points

  • While configuring the OAuth2 application, ensure that the required grants and scopes are maintained correctly
  • Missing grant or scope may result in 403 (Forbidden) errors in provisioning logs

For SAP IAG integration, the following grants can be maintained:

  • refresh_token
  • password

For more details, refer to:

  • Refresh Token Grant
  • Password Grant

Scopes to be Maintained

Below are the commonly required scopes:

 

Scope

Scope Description

identity.user.core.read

Read user core data

identity.user.coresensitive.read

Read core sensitive data

identity.user.emails.verified.writeonly

Write access for verified email

identity.user.enterprise.read

Read user enterprise data

identity.user.coreenterprise.writeonly

Write access to all core and enterprise fields except external ID

identity.user.externalID.writeonly

Write access to external ID only

identity.user.ids.read

Read user ID data

identity.user.sap.read

Read a user’s SAP Global ID. Used for intra SAP service communication

identity.user.sap.writeonly

Write a user’s SAP Global ID. Used for intra SAP service communication

spend.user.general.read

View spend user information

spend.user.general.writeonly

Change spend user information

travel.user.general.read

Read general Travel data: Travel approval manager, Travel Name, Rule Class, Groups, Org Unit, Travel Custom Fields

travel.user.private.read

Read private Travel data: CRS Name/GDS Sync ID, Travel Name Remark, Gender

user.provision.read

Request status of a provisioning request

user.provision.write

Provision a user

 

Required Permissions in SAP Concur

Ensure that the Concur administrator has the following permissions:

Activity

Permission

Configure OAuth2 authentication Application

Web Services Administrator

Generate Company Request Token and Company UUID

Web Services Administrator

User Creation and Maintenance

Employee Administrator

Permission Assignment and Administration

Role Administrator

 

Step 3: Destination Setup in BTP Subaccount

This step defines the connectivity configuration in SAP BTP to enable SAP IAG to communicate with SAP Concur APIs.

Create a destination for SAP Concur in the BTP subaccount where the SAP IAG service is subscribed.

Maintain the destination with the following details:

Property Key

Values

Name

CONCUR (or any other preferred name)

Type

HTTP

Description

Concur Destination (or any other preferred description)

URL

<Enter SAP Concur API URL> For example: https://us.api.concursolutions.com  (Refer to https://me.sap.com/notes/2914977/E for Concur URLs)

ProxyType

Internet

Authentication

OAuth2RefreshToken

Use mTLS for token retrieval

<Unchecked>

Client ID

Enter the generated Concur Client ID in Step 2.1

Client Secret

Enter the generated Concur Client Secret in Step 2.1

Token Service URL Type

Dedicated

Token Service URL

Enter the SAP Concur token URL in Step 2.2

CompanyEntityCode

Registered Company Entity Code can be obtained from SAP Concur team

CompanyUUID

Generated Company UUID in Step 2.2 (will be used as prefix to user ID in SAP Concur)

RefreshToken

Generated refresh token in Step 2.2

Use default client truststore

<Checked>

 

Important Note: The Company Entity Code is used as a prefix for the user ID during provisioning in SAP Concur from SAP IAG. As a result, the user ID is created in the format: <user_ID>@<CompanyEntityCode>

 

Step 4: Create an Application in SAP IAG

This step registers SAP Concur as a managed application in SAP IAG to enable access governance and provisioning.

Log in to SAP IAG and navigate to: Administration → Application

Click on the ‘+’ icon to create a new application and maintain the following details:

Field

Value

Application Name

CONCUR (or Any other preferred name)

Description

SAP Concur Application (or any other preferred description)

Application Type

SAP Concur

HCP Destination

<BTP destination created above. Provide exactly same name of BTP destination.>

 

Mandatory Custom Fields

Once the application is created, SAP IAG automatically creates four mandatory custom fields.
These can be verified under: Administration → Custom Fields

Name

Description

Label

Input Type

Data Type

Field Length

Required

Allowed values

COUNTRY

Spend User Country

Country

Input Text

String

2

Yes

A two-letter country code defined in ISO 3166-1 alpha-2

F(e.g. GB for UK, AU for Australia, US for United States of America, etc.)

LOCALE

Spend User Locale

Spend-User Locale

Input Text

String

5

Yes

Required when using Spend User extension. Valid locale from the list of configured locales as defined in [RFC5646]. But here for Concur, the locale will be an ISO 639 language code with an ISO 3166 country/region code, separated by a underscore (e.g., en_US, en_AU, de_DE, etc.)

REIMBURSEMENT_CURRENCY

SpendUser Reimbursement Currency

Reimbursement Currency

Input Text

String

3

Yes

Valid three digit currency code in the list of system reimbursement currencies. Ex. AUD, USD, etc.

REIMBURSEMENT_TYPE

SpendUser Reimbursement Type

Reimbursement Type

Input Text

String

20

Yes

The reimbursement type for the user. Supported values: ACCOUNTS_PAYABLE, ADP_PAYROLL, CONCUR_PAY, OTHER.

 

Important Points

  • These custom fields will appear in the access request form for SAP Concur
  • All four fields are mandatory for both new and existing users
  • Values must be provided in the correct format, otherwise provisioning will fail

Even though SAP IAG provides an option to edit or deactivate these fields under Custom Fields, it is recommended not to change them, as they are required for Concur provisioning.

 

Additional Note

SAP is introducing additional custom fields to support more Concur attributes. You can track updates here: SAP Roadmap Explorer – SAP IAG Concur Enhancements

 

Step 5: Run Repository Sync Job

This step helps to synchronize users and access (roles/profiles) from SAP Concur into SAP IAG for governance processes.

After creating the application, run the Repository Sync job to fetch data from SAP Concur.

Navigate to Administration → Job Scheduler and execute the Repository Sync job for the SAP Concur application

Once the job is completed successfully, user and access data from SAP Concur are synchronized to SAP IAG.

You can verify the synchronized data in:

  • Maintain User Data
  • Access Maintenance (use filters for SAP Concur)

User Data

  • SAP Concur user ID is displayed in the format: <user_id>@<CompanyEntityCode>
  • The <user_id> corresponds to the login name maintained in SAP CIS

Access Data

Under Access Maintenance, two types of access are available for SAP Concur:

  1. Concur Product: Represents Concur profiles such as Expense, Invoice, Reporting, Request, and Travel
  2. Single Roles: Represents authorization roles such as EXP_APPROVER, EXP_ATTENDEE_ADMIN, EXP_CONFIG_ADMIN_CLIENT, etc.

Both access types can be requested via SAP IAG. Additional attributes such as: Business Process, Subprocess, Criticality, Alias, Assignment Approvers etc. can be maintained to control workflow routing and approval determination.

Note:
Only users assigned with the IAG_RAA_DEFAULT group are available for role assignment approval.

 

Step 6: Configure Access Risk Ruleset

This step helps to enable risk analysis by importing and activating SAP Concur-specific segregation of duties rules.

For risk analysis, the standard GLOBAL ruleset for SAP Concur needs to be requested.

  • Create an SAP Support case for component: GRC-IAG-AA (Access Analysis Service)
  • Provide required access (IAS Administration) to SAP to upload the ruleset

Once the ruleset is uploaded:

  • A business function group SAP_CONCUR is created
  • All SAP Concur-related risks are available under this group

Ruleset Behavior

  • SAP Concur roles are standard and cannot be customized
  • Risks are defined based on conflicting role combinations

Example:

Post ruleset setup, validate the risk count against the business function group and run the Access Analysis job from the Job Scheduler.

After completion of Access Analysis job User-level risks can be checked in ‘Access Analysis’ application and role-level risks can be checked in ‘Access Maintenance’ application.

             

Step 7: User ID mapping

This step aligns user identities across systems to avoid duplicates and ensure accurate license consumption.

User ID mapping is important for license optimization in SAP IAG. The Monitored User List in Maintain User Data shows users consuming IAG licenses

Key Consideration

SAP Concur uses a different user ID format: <user_ID>@<prefix>

If multiple applications are integrated with SAP IAG:

  • The same user may appear as different users due to different ID formats
  • This can lead to duplicate license consumption

SAP IAG supports auto user ID mapping for most applications Refer: SAP Note 3553352 (Auto-Generated Entries in User ID Mapping) however, SAP Concur is not supported for auto-mapping

Hence to avoid duplicate license consumption user ID mapping must be maintained manually. This requires additional monitoring as part of ongoing operations for new user creation and also increases administrative effort.

 

How to Maintain Mapping

  • Navigate to: Administration → Maintain User ID Mapping
  • Mapping can be maintained: Individually or via Bulk upload through CSV file
  • Master User ID → Login name from user source and Mapped ID → SAP Concur User ID

 

Additional Notes

  • Other IAG services (Access Request, Workflow, Role Design, Access Analysis, Access Certification) work similarly to other applications
  • Privileged Access Management (PAM) Service is not supported for SAP Concur

 

Key Learnings and Limitations

Based on implementation experience, below are some important points to be aware of:

1. Provisioning Behavior (Asynchronous Processing)

Provisioning in SAP Concur works in an asynchronous manner.

  • Step 1: When the access request is submitted, the first run of the provisioning job shows the status as “In Process”
  • Step 2: In the next job run, the final status is updated as “Successful” or “Failed” based on the actual result

So, the provisioning result is not available immediately in the first run.

2. Manual User ID Mapping Required

As SAP Concur does not support automated user ID mapping:

  • Manual mapping is required in SAP IAG
  • Continuous monitoring is needed for new users

Without this, duplicate users may be created, leading to additional license consumption.

3. Mandatory Custom Fields

All four custom fields are mandatory:

  • COUNTRY
  • LOCALE
  • REIMBURSEMENT_CURRENCY
  • REIMBURSEMENT_TYPE

If any field is left empty or deactivated, provisioning will fail.

4. No Validation for Custom Fields

Custom fields accept only specific values (mentioned in step 4) and formats (case-sensitive).

However:

  • Validation is not enforced at the request submission stage, which may require additional checks during provisioning
  • Users can submit incorrect values
  • Request goes through full approval workflow
  • Failure happens only during provisioning job execution.

In addition:

  • Logs may require additional analysis to identify the exact issue
  • Administrators need to analyze logs to find the problem

 5. No Pre-population of Custom Fields

  • Custom fields need to be maintained for both new and existing users
  • There is no pre-filled data even for existing users

This requires proper end-user training before rollout.

 6. Partial User Creation in case of errors

If incorrect values are provided in custom fields user may still get created in SAP Concur but no profiles or roles are assigned

Such users are difficult to find using standard user search and may require support from SAP Concur functional team to verify at database level.

Users who exist in SAP Concur but do not have any profiles (Expense, Travel, etc.) will still appear in Maintain User Data and will be counted for SAP IAG licensing.

7. No Bulk Provisioning Support

SAP IAG currently does not support bulk user provisioning/deprovisioning for SAP Concur.

Recommended approach:

  • Use SAP ICS (Integration with SAP Concur Solutions) / SAP IPS for user creation with basic roles and/or user account termination/deactivation.
  • Use SAP IAG only for role-based access requests

8. Option for HR-Driven Provisioning

Integration with SuccessFactors can be used to trigger user creation/termination and manage lifecycle and provisioning automatically.

9. Refresh Token Handling

  • Refresh token used in BTP destination has a validity of 6 months
  • SAP IAG / SAP IPS automatically refreshes the token during provisioning job execution

No manual intervention is required in normal scenarios.

10. Standard Risk Rules May Need Adjustment

The Global ruleset for SAP Concur includes some risks which may not be relevant because SAP Concur has built-in controls for certain scenarios to prevent risks.

Such risks can be reviewed with business and deactivated, if required.

 

Overall, the integration is manageable, but attention to detail is important, especially around custom fields and user mapping. This guidance is based on practical implementation experience, highlighting what worked well and the challenges encountered during execution.

Going forward, SAP may address some of these challenges, such as improvements in custom field handling, automated user ID mapping, more relevant global rulesets, and clearer provisioning logs, which should simplify the integration further.

If you have faced similar challenges or have additional insights, feel free to share your experience in the comments.
